exercises.title: Game 47: Banikas vs. Gallagher (French League 2001)
exercises.description: The Four Pawns Attack main line. Black plays ...Bg4 and ...Qa5, leading to sharp tactical play where Black sacrifices material for active rooks. Black wins after White blunders under pressure.
exercises.textContext: Pages 123-126 of Starting Out: The King's Indian

preparsedJson.initial_moves: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 f4 O-O Nf3 c5 d5 e6 Be2 exd5 cxd5 Bg4 O-O Nbd7 Re1 Re8 h3 Bxf3 Bxf3 Qa5

moves.commentary: White develops the bishop, supporting the center.
Black starts the queenside counterplay, preparing ...b4.
Black knight heads for c4, putting pressure on White's queenside.
White strikes in the center, trying to open lines for his bishops.
Black knight invades c4, attacking the queen and b2.
White captures the knight on f6.
Black captures the bishop on e3.
White captures the bishop on g7, promoting a pawn temporarily.
(Diagram 4) Black has more than enough play. The rooks are active and White's bishop is restricted.
White tries to create counterplay on the kingside.
Black pushes the b-pawn, attacking the knight.
Black recaptures with the queen, attacking the knight.
Black queen switches to the kingside, threatening infiltration.
White captures the pawn on g6.
White retreats the knight to defend. This is the first new move of the game.
Black activates the rook, preparing to double.
Black supports the e4 square and restricts White.
White plays a passive bishop move.
Black king captures the g7 pawn, eliminating the potential danger.
White queen moves to the side, looking for counterplay but leaving the king.
White queen captures the a6 pawn.
Black rook invades the second rank, threatening f2.
(Diagram 5) Black plays the winning move. If 32 Rxe1 Qxf2+ 33 Kh2 Qxe1 wins.
Black king moves to defend. White resigns.
White tries to prevent queenside expansion.
White must exchange to prevent knight infiltration.

sidelines.name: Alternative 16 Bf2
sidelines.description: White plays 16 Bf2. Moves from Kozul-Nunn, Wijk aan Zee 1991.

moves.commentary: This keeps the bishop out of b5.

exercises.title: Game 48: Vaisser vs. Bauer (French Cup 1992)
exercises.description: The Four Pawns Attack, 9...Re8 10 e5 line. Black plays aggressively, leading to a rook sacrifice on f5 and a draw by perpetual check after both players navigate sharp tactics.
exercises.textContext: Pages 126-127 of Starting Out: The King's Indian

preparsedJson.initial_moves: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 f4 O-O Nf3 c5 d5 e6 Be2 exd5 cxd5 Re8

moves.commentary: White pushes the e-pawn, starting the complications. Alternatives 10 Qc2 and 10 Nd2 are also possible.
White develops the bishop, attacking the queen. 12 O-O is an alternative.
Black queen moves to b6, pressuring b2.
White exchanges knights. 14 d6 is a sharp alternative.
(Diagram 6) White develops the bishop, targeting f7. White has a strong attack.
Black develops the bishop to f5, blocking the attack.
White knight maneuvers, targeting d6. 16 d6! was necessary.
Black forces the knight back or forces d6.
White pushes d-pawn, sacrificing a piece for attack.
White sacrifices the bishop on f7.
White sacrifices the rook on f5.
White queen joins the attack with check.
White bishop checks. Alternative 21 Qxh7 led to draw in Vaisser-Apicella.
Black queen captures the d6 pawn.
(Diagram 7) White threatens mate or invasion. How should Black defend?
Bauer's new move, defending and forcing a draw. 25...Ra6 was old theory.
White checks again, draw by repetition agreed.

sidelines.name: Alternative 10 Qc2?
sidelines.description: A mistake by White, allowing Black to win a pawn.

sidelines.name: Alternative 12 O-O
sidelines.description: White plays 12 O-O instead of 12 Bg5.

moves.commentary: Black captures e5, leading to similar play.

sidelines.name: Alternative 14 d6
sidelines.description: An exciting variation leading to a draw by perpetual check.

sidelines.name: Alternative 21 Comparison (Vaisser-Apicella 1996)
sidelines.description: White plays 21 Collection: 21精Qxh7 instead of 21 Bh6+.

sidelines.name: Alternative 25...Ra6
sidelines.description: Old theory, leading to a win for White.

exercises.title: Game 49: Blokh vs. Kichev (corr USSR 1991)
exercises.description: The Four Pawns Attack, 9...b5?! line. Black tries an early queenside strike, but White's central push 10 e5! proves strong. White coordinates his pieces well, leading to an exchange win.
exercises.textContext: Pages 127-128 of Starting Out: The King's Indian

preparsedJson.initial_moves: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 f4 O-O Nf3 c5 d5 e6 Be2 exd5 cxd5

moves.commentary: Black strikes on the queenside early. This is a risky line.
(Diagram 8) White pushes e5!, striking in the center. Alternatives like 10 Bxb5 allow Black active play.
Black accepts the central challenge. 10...Nfd7 was Frolov's move.
Black blocks with pawn. 12...Qb6 is also possible but less effective.
Black rook active on e-file. Alternatives 14...Bxg5 and 14...Bf5 favor White.
(Diagram 9) White queen threatens f7. Black is forced into a lost ending.
White rook to b7. Black resigns.

sidelines.name: Alternative 10 Bxb5
sidelines.description: White captures on b5 instead of e5!.

sidelines.name: Alternative 10...Nfd7
sidelines.description: Frolov's move, but White obtains advantage.

exercises.title: Theory: 6...c5 Early Deviations
exercises.description: Analysis of early deviations after 6...c5 in the Four Pawns Attack, focusing on the moves after 8...exd5.
exercises.textContext: Page 129 of Starting Out: The King's Indian

preparsedJson.initial_moves: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 f4 O-O Nf3 c5 d5 e6 Be2 exd5

moves.commentary: (Diagram 10) Alternative capture 9 exd5 (Game 50). Main line is 9 cxd5. 9 e5?! is speculative.

sidelines.name: Alternative 9 e5?!
sidelines.description: A speculative move that was popular in the 1980s but discarded because of 9...Ne4!.

moves.commentary: White recaptures. 10 Nxd5 is also possible but favors Black.
